_comment: STRICT CONFIGURATION: High threshold for inventive step. Acts as a critical EPO Examiner. Question titles and strictness tags have been entirely removed to prevent semantic bias.

Q1.text: [Q1] Does the prior art share any general purpose, utility, or broad technical goal with the claimed invention, even if the specific application differs?

Q2.text: [Q2] Does the prior art produce technical effects or outcomes that are at all comparable or functionally analogous to the invention?

Q3.text: [Q3] Does the prior art belong to the same field, or a neighboring/analogous field that the skilled person would routinely consult?

Q4.text: [Q4] Is there a documented, substantive legal conflict regarding the Common General Knowledge, or is it merely standard technical background?

Q5.text: [Q5] Is the Common General Knowledge backed by basic standard textbooks, rather than isolated patent literature?

Q6.text: [Q6] Is the Person Skilled in the Art (PSA) correctly defined as an average practitioner aware of all standard techniques in this broad field?

Q7.text: [Q7] Is the skilled person presumed to be capable of routine work, standard optimization, and straightforward trial-and-error without exercising inventive skill?

Q8.text: [Q8] Is the skilled person presumed to possess comprehensive knowledge of all basic engineering and scientific principles in their field?

Q9.text: [Q9] Would the skilled person have had unrestricted access to routinely find and read the cited prior art?

Q10.text: [Q10] Given the complexity, should the skilled person actually be considered a multi-disciplinary research or production team rather than a single individual?

Q11.text: [Q11] Can any reasonable, plausible candidate document be identified to serve as the Closest Prior Art?

Q12.text: [Q12] Is the selected CPA a plausible starting point? Note: It does not need to be the absolute 'most' promising, so long as it addresses a related technical problem.

Q13.text: [Q13] Is there any implicit suggestion, logical incentive, or standard design trend that would prompt the combination of these documents?

Q14.text: [Q14] Do the secondary documents belong to a field the skilled person would logically consult when faced with this technical problem?

Q15.text: [Q15] Would the skilled person, using routine engineering skills, have seen a practical motive to combine these teachings?

Q16.text: [Q16] Is there any basic structural or functional compatibility that allows these disclosures to be associated?

Q17.text: [Q17] Do the distinguishing features strictly contribute to a purely technical solution, entirely avoiding mathematical, business, or administrative elements?

Q19.text: [Q19] Do the features rely heavily on non-technical exclusions (e.g., algorithms, business rules, UI presentation) to achieve their outcome?

Q20.text: [Q20] Is the technical problem merely bypassed or avoided via a non-technical administrative or software rule?

Q21.text: [Q21] Does the implementation require highly specific, non-routine technical adaptations that exceed standard programming or engineering?

Q22.text: [Q22] Is the specific technical effect explicitly proven and demonstrated in the application, rather than merely claimed?

Q23.text: [Q23] Is the invention strictly limited to a concrete, physical, real-world technical application?

Q24.text: [Q24] Is the application strictly directed toward a recognized, objective technical engineering purpose?

Q25.text: [Q25] Are the features strictly and narrowly limited by the claim language to ensure the technical purpose is always achieved?

Q26.text: [Q26] Is the resulting technical effect completely astonishing, synergistic, and entirely unpredictable to the skilled person?

Q27.text: [Q27] Is the problem described in strictly objective, measurable technical terms?

Q28.text: [Q28] Was this claimed solution the absolute only viable technical path forward, devoid of alternative standard choices?

Q29.text: [Q29] Is the claimed technical effect rigorously credible and consistently reproducible across the entire breadth of the claim?

Q30.text: [Q30] Does the independent claim explicitly include every single essential technical feature required to achieve the effect?

Q31.text: [Q31] Is the disclosure entirely sufficient, allowing the skilled person to reproduce the invention without undue burden or inventive effort?

Q32.text: [Q32] Do the features interact to produce a genuine, unpredictable synergistic effect, rather than a mere aggregation of known effects?

Q33.text: [Q33] Do the features work together in a strictly interdependent, mutually supportive functional relationship?

Q34.text: [Q34] Is the objective technical problem explicitly supported and clearly derivable from the application as originally filed?

Q36.text: [Q36] Is the scope of the patent claim strictly and narrowly aligned only with the proven technical contribution, ensuring it does not cover any non-working embodiments?

Q38.text: [Q38] Does the formulation of the problem contain absolutely no pointers, hints, or implicit references to the claimed solution?

Q39.text: [Q39] Would the skilled person, using routine engineering and optimization, have been motivated to modify the prior art to arrive at the invention (would)? Note: Explicit pointers are not required if the modification is standard practice.

Q40.text: [Q40] Does the invention involve a disadvantageous modification that sacrifices a known benefit?

Q41.text: [Q41] Was this disadvantage and its resulting drawbacks a foreseeable trade-off to the skilled person?

Q42.text: [Q42] Was the disadvantage adequately compensated for by a genuinely unexpected, disproportionate technical advantage?

Q43.text: [Q43] Does the invention relate to biotechnology?

Q45.text: [Q45] Were the biotech results highly predictable using standard, established scientific models?

Q46.text: [Q46] Would the skilled person have embarked on this project with a reasonable expectation of success based on existing literature?

Q47.text: [Q47] Were the antibodies generated using routine, standard techniques already known in the art?

Q48.text: [Q48] Did the creation of the antibodies require overcoming severe, documented technical prejudices or difficulties?

Q49.text: [Q49] Does the invention merely fill an obvious gap using routine engineering choices that would naturally occur to the skilled person?

Q50.text: [Q50] Does the invention merely substitute one well-known technical equivalent for another predictable component?

Q51.text: [Q51] Is the invention just a new use that relies entirely on the inherent, predictable properties of a known material?

Q52.text: [Q52] Does the invention simply apply a known technique from a closely analogous technical field?

Q53.text: [Q53] Does the invention rely strictly on the use or combination of known, off-the-shelf devices?

Q54.text: [Q54] Is the invention a mere juxtaposition of known devices, each performing their standard function without synergy?

Q55.text: [Q55] Does the invention simply substitute a newer, standard material into an older, known device?

Q56.text: [Q56] Does the invention result from a purely arbitrary choice between equally suitable, known alternatives?

Q57.text: [Q57] Does the invention consist of optimizing standard parameters through routine, trial-and-error design procedures?

Q58.text: [Q58] Is the invention a predictable, straightforward extrapolation of existing state-of-the-art trends?

Q59.text: [Q59] Does the invention merely involve selecting a routine chemical compound from a broadly defined known class?

Q99.text: [Q99] Is the objective technical problem formulated completely neutrally, without giving away any part of the solution?

Q100.text: [Q100] Does the invention possess strictly defined, unambiguous technical features that distinguish it structurally from the CPA?

Q101.text: [Q101] Do these specific features provide a purely technical contribution to the art, devoid of administrative rules?

Q102.text: [Q102] Is the technical effect genuinely unexpected and entirely unpredictable from the state of the art?

Q103.text: [Q103] Is the problem framed in strictly measurable, objective technical terms?

Q104.text: [Q104] Was the claimed solution the sole viable technical path, excluding any routine alternatives?

Q105.text: [Q105] Is the technical effect completely credible and consistently reproducible across the whole scope of the claim?

Q106.text: [Q106] Does the claim explicitly recite all technical features required to solve the problem?

Q107.text: [Q107] Does the patent provide exhaustive detail enabling the skilled person to reproduce the invention without undue burden?

Q200.text: [Q200] Is the objective technical problem explicitly derived from the text of the application as originally filed?

Q201.text: [Q201] Is the claim scope narrowly tailored to exactly match the specific technical contribution shown in the examples?

Q202.text: [Q202] Does the problem formulation improperly rely on hindsight knowledge of the invention?

Q203.text: [Q203] Would the skilled person have routinely modified the art to arrive at the invention in the expectation of improvement (would)?
